data.title: PEJ: Thematic ETF With Good Fundamentals, But Bad Track Record
data.description:
PEJ strategy

Invesco Leisure and Entertainment ETF (NYSEARCA:PEJ) was launched on 06/23/2005 and tracks the Dynamic Leisure & Entertainment Intellidex℠ Index. It has 32 holdings and a total expense ratio of 0.58%. As described in the prospectus by Invesco, the underlying index selects companies that…

...are engaged principally in the design, production or distribution of goods or services in the leisure and entertainment industries. These companies may include hospitality industry companies such as hotels, restaurants and bars, cruise lines, casinos, and all other recreation and amusement businesses; as well as entertainment programming companies engaged in the production of motion pictures, music by recording artists, programming for radio and television, related post-production and movie theaters.

The portfolio turnover rate in the most recent fiscal year was 163%, which is very high for such a thematic fund.

PEJ portfolio

The portfolio has about 8% of asset value in large companies, 52% in mid-caps and 41% in small and micro-caps. About 50% of assets is in the consumer discretionary sector and 36% in communication services. PEJ also has marginal exposure in consumer staples and industrials.

At sub-sector level in the GICS classification, all the Consumer Discretionary weight is in a single category: Hotels, Restaurants & Leisure. The Communication Services part is concentrated in the Entertainment industry (28%), with also minor exposure in Media and Interactive Media & Services. The Consumer Staples part is exclusively in distribution and retail, while airlines make all of the small allocation in industrials.

The portfolio is quite concentrated. The top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent 47% of asset value and the four heaviest positions weigh between 5% and 6%.

data.title: China Automotive Systems: Revenue And Profitability Growth Not Converting To Cash Flows
data.description:
China Automotive Systems, Inc. (NASDAQ:CAAS) has seen its share price recover in 2024, following the sharp decline last year.

Despite the steady revenue growth since 2021, the company hasn’t been able to turn this improvement into stronger cash flows, which naturally, raised some eyebrows.

Therefore, in this article, I considered reviewing the recent financial performance of the company, with an emphasis on the headwinds, challenges, and risks that the company is facing.

In the outlook section, I will provide the rationale behind my Hold rating, including a deep dive into valuation and the fundamentals of the company.

For now, I would like to start with a brief company overview section for those readers new to this stock.

Company Overview

China Automotive, as its name suggests, is a Chinese based supplier of power steering systems for the automotive industry. Their power gears are used in passenger vehicles and commercial trucks.

Their major customers include large automotive manufacturers in China (BYD Auto, Geely, and Chery) and international companies like Stellantis and Ford.

The company operates mainly in China through several subsidiaries that specialize in different areas of the automotive steering market. I considered including a breakdown of revenue per business segment below.

Ownership-wise, the latest 14A shows 65% ownership of common stock across all 9 directors and executive officers. In my view, a very respectable amount of skin in the game.

Headwinds

I'd like to start with dessert.

One of the factors that raised my eyebrow in their last earnings results was the disconnection between their strong revenue growth and the decline in net income.

Net sales were up by 15.4% YoY, operating income was up by 28%, however, net income declined by 32% in the same period, falling from $10.5 million to $7.1 million.

By looking at the income statement, it seems that this decline was driven by unfavorable foreign exchange rates.

The company reported a net financial expense of $0.7 million, a big contrast to the net financial income of $4.0 million in Q2 2023.

In other words, we are talking about a $4.7 million negative swing in the company’s financial results due to foreign exchange volatility, which is quite concerning, in my view.

The company has substantial exposure to foreign currencies, due to its revenue from export markets like North America and Brazil.

Looking at the charts of the Brazilian Real vs the Chinese Yuan Renminbi and the US Dollar vs Chinese Yuan Renminbi the company's foreign exchange loss in Q2 makes sense.

Despite this high volatility, I remain highly disappointed that the company has no foreign exchange forward contracts to hedge against foreign exchange risks. Back in 2023, the company entered into foreign exchange forward contracts to mitigate the impact of exchange rate fluctuations between the RMB and USD. However, this strategy has not been extended to 2024, leaving the company exposed to exchange rate fluctuations.

Moving on to other pressures, operating expenses increased by 40.7% YoY. I am not overly concerned about this increase considering that operating income saw a decent improvement in the last quarter of 28.9%.

However, I am slightly more concerned about the risks associated with their transition towards electric power systems. As of today, the traditional steering product market is still a large portion of the company's revenue, accounting for over 65% of net sales.

However, as the industry gradually moves towards more electrified and autonomous vehicles, the demand for traditional steering components may stagnate or decline, particularly in more advanced markets like North America and Europe.

That's not the situation today, since sales of the traditional systems grew by 7.5% compared to last year. However, in the next 5-10 years, the company may need to shift its focus from traditional power systems to electric ones. Otherwise, their revenue could take a significant hit.

Outlook

I'd like to start with price action.

If we look at the share price over the last 2 years, there is a mixed performance, with a significant increase in Q1 2023, followed by a decline in the same year.

From a technical analysis perspective, there is an upward trend line that seems to be quite reliable.

From a valuation perspective, most of the valuation ratios seem to be trading close to the 5-year minimum, except for P/CF and P/FCF.

So, let's have a closer look at the cash flow statement and try to understand why these two ratios look so bad.

Operating cash flow dropped by 200% in the second quarter, but what's even more worrying is that free cash flow plunged by 700%.

As mentioned in the previous section, the increase in operating expenses and the decline in financial income due to unfavorable forex rates played a big role in this decline.

In addition, accounts and notes receivable saw a significant increase of $20.5 million. I am concerned about this increase in receivables, as the revenue growth is not leading to an increase in cash, indicating either extended credit terms to customers or delays in collecting payments.

On top of that, income taxes paid went up by $600k compared to last year, adding to the overall decline.

If we zoom out, and we consider a yearly timeframe, the trend in operating and free cash flow does not look favorable, despite the revenue growth.

My investment style doesn't align with this trend since I put more emphasis on cash flow than revenue, except for companies in their early growth phase.

Moving on to the balance sheet, the company has negative net debt, with cash & equivalents sitting at $148.4 million.

The liquidity ratios look ok. Nothing spectacular, but no major red flags.

Ratio Value
Current Ratio 1.5
Quick Ratio 1.2
Cash Ratio 0.4

The cash ratio is low, mainly due to the $162 million in accounts payable.

Moving on to insider buying activity, over the past 12 months there was only one transaction made by the CFO, Li Jie, totaling $19k in November 2023.

Considering the small amount purchased by the CFO, and the lack of recent insider buying, I remain disappointed. On the bright side, there were no insider selling transactions.

The activist activity has been silent since 2018, and the amount of shares owned by institutional investors has not changed drastically in the last quarter. However, the put/call ratio increased by 36% in Q2, indicating some pessimism among 13F filers.

Conclusion

To wrap up, I find that China Automotive presents a mixed outlook.

On the bright side, the company has shown solid revenue growth consistently since 2021. Operating income followed this trend, however, the net income of the company seems to be quite volatile due to foreign exchange rates.

I appreciate the fact that management has a significant ownership stake in the company, but I disagree with their decision not to hedge against foreign exchange fluctuations in 2024.

On the negative side, the 700% YoY drop in free cash flow, despite revenue growth, points out to operational inefficiencies, particularly with increasing accounts receivable. Additionally, their transition to electric power systems remains a concern, especially if the company fails to pivot toward electrification as the automotive industry moves in that direction.

On top of that, the lack of insider buying and the increasing put/call ratio shows that both management and institutional investors are being cautious.

To conclude, while I see decent revenue growth, I don’t see the same improvement in their cash flow statement. Therefore, I’m keeping a Hold rating until their cash flows improve and, ideally, insiders start buying shares.

data.title: Johnson & Johnson: Q3 Earnings Highlights Resilience And New Growth
data.description:
It has been nearly a year since my last Johnson & Johnson (NYSE:JNJ) “J&J” article, where I highlighted their Q3 2023 earnings that beat the Street’s projections with $21.4B in revenue, which was up 6.8% year-over-year. At that time, it looked as if the company’s MedTech division was going to be a primary growth driver, along with Darzalex and Abiomed. That quarter was a critical time for J&J following their strategic spin-off of Kenuve consumer health business while still dealing with the ongoing Talc litigation. In addition, J&J was facing the looming threat of STELARA biosimilar competition for one their blockbuster drugs. Despite the murky outlook, I remained optimistic thanks to J&J’s pipeline and their track record of excellence. Now, we are here a year later looking at their Q3 earnings report that revealed a beat on EPS and revenue that was once again fueled by the company's blockbusters Darzalex, Invega Sustenna, and STELARA. That being said, the company has adjusted their full-year earnings guidance following their V-Wave acquisition for $1.7B amongst other headwinds. After a brief sell-off, JNJ was able to recover in the trading session and is now trading above my Buy Threshold of $163 per share. Although I am still apprehensive about the impact of STELARA generics in the coming quarters, I believe their Q3 earnings showed the company’s resilience and some new growth opportunities. As a result, I am looking to add to my JNJ position in the near term.

I intend to review the company’s Q3 earnings and will provide my views on the quarter. Then, I will highlight some of J&J's new growth opportunities. In addition, I will point out some notable risks investors need to be aware of. Finally, I reveal my plans for managing my JNJ in the coming days and weeks.

Q3 Earnings Review

Taking a look at J&J’s Q3 earnings their total revenue came in at $22.5B, which was up 5.4% year-over-year. In terms of EPS, J&J publicized that their non-GAAP EPS came in at $2.42. The company attributed their growth to the Innovative Medicine segment, which pulled in $14.58B, up 0.8% quarter-over-quarter and 4.9% year-over-year. I will highlight that once again, STELARA was able to beat analyst expectations and was the primary contributor throwing $2.68B into the coffers.

Again, J&J’s Darzalex was a heavy hitter posting $3.02B for Q3, which beat the Street's forecast of $2.94B. In addition, other oncology drugs such as ARVYKTI, TECVAYLI, and TALVEY are showing growth. ARVYKTI hit $286M in sales, which was up 87.6% year-over-year and 53.2% quarter-over-quarter. In addition, TECVAYLI has made strong gains in the multiple myeloma market with sales growing 21.4% year-over-year to hit $135M. J&J also highlighted ERLEADA’s growth, up 26.3% year-over-year, as it continues to claim more of the metastatic castrate-sensitive prostate cancer market.

Meanwhile, immunology had mixed results, with TREMFYA posting 14.3% growth as it makes inroads into the psoriasis and psoriatic arthritis markets. The company’s neuroscience products are also contributing with SPRAVATO recording 55.3% growth. J&J’s OPSUMIT and UPTRAVI posted solid growth of 17.4% and 15.2%, respectively.

In addition, the company’s MedTech segment recorded a solid performance as the number of procedures increased and they rolled out new products. MedTech hit $7.89B in sales, growing 0.8% quarter-over-quarter increase and 5.2% year-over-year. The company reported a recovery in the number of surgical procedures driving demand for their medical devices. Although the segment did miss the Street’s mark of $8.03B, the growing demand does bode well for J&J’s MedTech business going forward. In addition, J&J pointed to their acquisitions of Shockwave and Abiomed helped drive double-digit growth.

Turning to the company’s vision products, ACUVUE OASYS’s price adjustments led to 4.7% growth, and their TECNIS products saw some growth.

Looking at J&J’s regional sales figures, their U.S. sales were scribed in at $12.57B, which was up 2.7% from Q2 and 7.6% year-over-year. On the other hand, the company pulled in $9.88B from international markets, which was down 1.2% from last quarter, but up 2.2% year-over-year. Notably, the company believes the drop from Q2 was mostly due to currency and weaker sales in Europe. However, the company did point out that the year-over-year growth came from emerging markets grabbing Innovative Medicine and MedTech products.

The company’s Q3 earnings encouraged them to update their full-year 2024 guidance with sales coming in between $89.4B-$89.8B, which is up from $89.2B-$89.6B. J&J also updated their adjusted operational EPS guidance to $9.86-$9.96, which is down from $10.00-$10.10. I must point out that the company said they revised their EPS due to the V-Wave acquisition, and talc litigation costs.

In terms of their finances, J&J closed out Q3 with ~$20B in cash and marketable securities thanks to $14B in free cash flow. In addition, the company has about $36B in debt, leading to a net debt of about $16B.

My Take

For me, J&J’s Q3 results highlighted their ability to frequently outperform the Street’s expectations, despite some of the looming concerns and operating in a challenging environment. Beating the Street’s EPS estimates by $0.21 and surpassing revenue forecasts by $330M shows just how their leading products are in control of their markets, helping the company log $22.5B in total revenue for the quarter. STELARA and Darzalex are the company’s big hitters who are still capable of carrying the company forward. However, I’m also encouraged by their MedTech segment, which posted a solid 5.2% year-over-year growth. I am also encouraged about the company’s outlook on how their Shockwave and Abiomed acquisitions could have a strong impact in the near future.

Indeed, J&J still has plenty of challenges including generics, currency headwinds, and ongoing litigation, but I see J&J's decision to adjust their guidance and maintain strong cash flow as strong signals that they are optimistic about their near-term performance. Furthermore, the fact that they’re still paying out $8.8B in dividends year-to-date reassures me they’re committed to shareholders while not stunting growth.

Overall, I don’t see any major red flags coming out this earnings, and the company’s beat should tell you that the lingering concerns are not hindering their performance.

Looking For Growth

J&J's Darzalex and STELARA, have set a solid foundation for the company to continue to grow in the near term. However, I have to recognize that the upcoming generic competition for STELARA could deteriorate branded sales, faster than Darzalex can grow. Thankfully, I believe that J&J’s overall pharmaceutical portfolio still has pending growth. The company’s CARVYKTI and TECVALYI have reported strong double-digit year-over-year growth and are positioned to grab more of the oncology market. I’m also excited about some of J&J’s new products and their ability to be immediate contributors. I must also acknowledge that RYBREVANT and TREMFYA are now approved and could bulk up their oncology and immunology revenues. I'm also champing at the bit to see the sales disclosures for TALVEY, as well as the anticipated sales for RYBREVANT.

Although J&J's MedTech segment slightly underperformed in Q3, it still holds a lot of promise with their surgical systems and cardiovascular devices, which should deliver significant sales in the future. The ongoing integration of V-Wave’s cardiac device technologies should start to show traction after adding some short-term pressure to earnings. The company should also see some revenue growth from REMICADE and SIMPONI in Europe as distribution rights are set to return to J&J in Q4 of this year.

I am not the only one expecting growth from J&J… the Street is expecting the company to report growth in the coming quarters with a solid single-digit to low double-digit revenue growth from Q4 2024 to Q4 2026.

Admittedly, the quarterly EPS estimates are expected to stagnate for a few quarters, but analysts are projecting the numbers to improve in Q3 of next year and will continue to grow through 2026.

Zooming out, we can see that analysts are expecting J&J to report single-digit growth for the remainder of the decade for both EPS and revenue.

Certainly, single-digit growth is not going to justify a premium valuation, however, I think it does repudiate the narrative that STELARA generics and the talc litigation are going derail J&J’s growth. Yes, both of those issues have most likely hurt the company’s growth trajectory, but the company can still leverage their pharmaceutical strengths, innovative therapies, and strategic acquisitions to drive growth for the remainder of the decade.

Risks To Consider

Despite my bullish outlook for J&J, I still have to acknowledge a few risks that could hurt the company's long-term outlook. First, I must acknowledge their talc litigation, which will most likely continue to weigh on the ticker as investors remain apprehensive about the fallout from potential settlements.

Furthermore, the costs of integrating V-Wave are going to be a concern. While this acquisition helped contribute to the top line, it has also increased expenses. The company is still expecting an impact in 2025, but any setbacks in rollouts and R&D could hurt profitability. As a result, J&J needs to show that they can quickly and seamlessly integrate V-Wave into their existing operations in order to start hitting the Street’s expectations for their MedTech segment.

Last but not least, the impending generic competition for STELARA is a serious concern. In fact, the drug’s Q3 sales experienced a decline of 5.7% due to biosimilar competition in Europe, and investors should expect a similar challenge in the U.S. in 2025. Investors should be prepared for STELARA sales numbers to dip, which will require the company to rely heavily on their newer therapies to fill the gap and maintain the growth trajectory.

Despite these risks, I maintain my conviction level for J&J at a strong 5 out of 5. I believe the company has the resilience and strategic vision to navigate these challenges effectively. Furthermore, I still view J&J as a “Healthy Dividend” stock, reflecting its commitment to returning capital to shareholders even amidst these headwinds.

My Plan

Indeed, the launch of STELARA generics is inevitable and will be a major blow to the company's portfolio. In addition, the dark cloud from the talc litigation will likely prevent the ticker from outperforming its peers. It is possible that STELARA is able to fend off generics longer than Street believes, and maybe the talc litigation will finally come to an end sooner than expected. Moreover, the company's next batch of growth drivers might be able to help offset some of the projected drop in revenue from STELARA. Plus, it looks as if Darzalex will continue to be the main driver in the near term with projected peak sales of $13B to roughly $16B by 2029. As a result, I will be looking to add to my JNJ position in the near term if the ticker is able to drop below my “Buy Threshold” of $163 per share.

Looking at the Daily Chart, we can see JNJ has rebounded from support around $161 and is approaching resistance near $165. The chart shows several indicators are pointing to a potential move higher now that the share price has climbed above the anchored-VWAP and the “Go-No-Go” is now neutral. On the other hand, the TW Pivot is suggesting a potential bearish reversal is on the horizon, so I am going to wait to see if the ticker can break above the downtrend ray and will retest support before clicking the buy button. If that occurs, I will make an upsized addition and then will revisit the ticker at the end of the month to see if I have to update my Buy and Sell Targets.

data.title: Ford Q3 Earnings Preview: Why I'm Steering Clear Before The Report
data.description:
Ford Motor (NYSE:F) has its much-awaited Q3 earnings release coming up on October 28, but I won't be buying going into the report. Ford is one of those divisive stocks that people often refer to as either deep value or a value trap. I understand why some may believe it's very undervalued based on its low valuation, high dividend yield, and innovation efforts. However, it's just not that appealing of an investment to me, even when considering its 5.9x forward P/E ratio.

It faces intense competition from Tesla (TSLA) and Chinese competitors, has declining gross profit margins, and unimpressive profitability metrics. Further, the outlook for US auto sector is not that great. Additionally, it can technically buy back shares at a low valuation, which I'm sure lots of shareholders would like, but it's choosing not to. I'll explain why I don't like that later on.

Overall, it doesn't look attractive going into earnings, especially when considering previous post-earnings reactions. I rate F stock as a Hold.

Ford Q3-2024 Earnings Preview

For the upcoming Q3 report, analysts expect $0.48 in EPS and $41.87 billion in revenue. Ford's track record of beating EPS estimates is a bit lumpy, with four misses in the past 12 quarters. This isn't horrible, but there are plenty of companies out there that consistently beat estimates, and I'm more confident in those firms.

It's a similar story with revenue misses, with 5 misses in the last 12 quarters (3 of them being in the last 4 quarters).

But what I don't like is the recent reactions to Ford's earnings reports. As you can see in the image below, there have been more negative earnings reactions than positive ones. The negative reactions also tend to be more extreme than the positive ones. I'm aware that those are just historical figures, but that's not something I want to be exposed to going into earnings, especially if I'm not 100% confident in the company.

Next, for the upcoming quarter, investors will be watching for improvements regarding warranty reserves because that was a problem in the last quarter. Specifically, in its Q2 press release, Ford stated the following: "Profitability was affected by an increase in warranty reserves, though efforts to lift the quality of new products are starting to pay off, with positive implications for customer satisfaction and Ford’s operating performance." Let's hope their efforts actually pay off here.

The Competition Is Tough

Ford faces tough competition, particularly in the EV space, from Tesla (TSLA) and Chinese automakers like BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF). This obviously pressures Ford to lower costs and improve its EVs. Tesla still dominates the EV market in the US, and its aggressive pricing cuts and innovation have made it hard for Ford to compete. Ford isn't even listed in the chart below.

In the Q2-2024 earnings call, Ford's CEO, Jim Farley, said, "We clearly see China and Tesla as the cost benchmark." Now, Ford has a goal of making vehicles "priced under $40,000 or even $30,000," which they can achieve by building smaller cars (smaller cars = smaller battery = lower cost and higher margins). But can it do that profitably eventually? Ford still expects $5.0-5.5 billion in losses for its Model e division this year. How long will it take to become profitable in this segment and achieve economies of scale?

Notably, S&P Global warned recently:

S&P sees significant competitive pressure in 2025 and 2026 for all automakers. S&P pointed to the large market share losses this year for Tesla's (NASDAQ:TSLA) Model 3 despite multiple price cuts. "This could slow revenue growth and delay profitability parity for EVs relative to legacy products possibly beyond 2027, especially for issuers that do not achieve economies of scale.

Thus, things aren't looking good for Ford. Plus, Chinese automakers offer great value for their products, not just in China but globally. Based on this image below, Ford has a lot of work to do to catch up to the competition, with the BYD Sea Lion 07 coming in at a cheaper price and offering more range.

Also, let's not forget that Tesla will likely release its Model 2 next year, which will be a lower-cost vehicle, adding to the competition. Even though the Model 2 wasn't mentioned in Tesla's recent robotaxi event, that could be because they didn't want to cannibalize sales of their other models like the Model 3.

Ford's Profitability Isn't Impressive

One thing I don't like about Ford is its relatively low ROE and its declining and low gross profit margins in recent years (due to intense competition).

Its gross margin is at a relatively low 8% for the trailing 12 months, and the trend hasn't been strong recently. I'm aware that other major automakers have seen their margins decline, but they're not as low as Ford's (example: Tesla's GPM comes in at 17.7%, while General Motors' (GM) margin is 11.8%). But either way, I don't like that for either of those 3 companies. It's a highly competitive industry for all, making it unattractive.

I also don't like how its ROE is inconsistent and hasn't been high in recent years, with the exception for 2021. Its current ROE is 8.8%, lower than its cost of equity of 12.23% (calculated using the CAPM model), meaning that it's returning less than the cost of its equity.

The Valuation Still Isn't Low Enough

Although Ford is trading at a forward P/E ratio of 5.9x for 2024 and 5.8x for 2025, it's not low enough for me because of the risks associated with competition. Ford also expects $7.5-8.5 billion in adjusted free cash flow for the year (they raised guidance by $1 billion), giving it a forward price/FCF of 5.52x at the midpoint.

Still, the higher FCF guide was due to "lower-than-planned CapEx." But is this just a temporary drop in planned CapEx? I think that Ford will have to maintain high CapEx going forward to stay competitive. Additionally, analysts expect Ford's FCF to fall to around $6 billion (per simplywall.st) by 2025.

Anyway, what I'm getting at here is that with these FCF estimates, the dividend yield won't be high enough. In the Q2 investor presentation, Ford said that it is "targeting a consistent return of 40% - 50% of adj. FCF to shareholders."

If it pays, say, 45% of its FCF in dividends (if a special dividend is instituted again), then the annualized dividend per share will be about $0.906 per share using the current share count of 3.975 billion. That gives you a yield of 8.16%, but the growth prospects for this dividend aren't great, especially if FCF falls next year. Combined with a cost of equity of over 12%, I just don't see the value -- at least not relative to other stocks that I've given Buy ratings to.

Lack Of Buybacks Won't Help The Valuation

And it's not as if management will buy back a notable number of shares to boost the valuation. That's because it can't -- again, because of the competitive nature of the industry. Ford has to invest heavily in the future to remain relevant, to the point where it doesn't have much money left over for buybacks. If the industry was less competitive, or if Ford had a better competitive advantage, it would have more money left over. That's what I don't like about it.

In the Q2 earnings call, Adam Jonas asked Ford's CEO if he thought the stock was undervalued, to which he replied "Yes." Then, Adam Jonas asked why the company isn't repurchasing shares if that's the case, asking if the CEO sees better opportunities to use capital.

Here's what Ford's CEO said:

Yes, we do [see better uses of capital]. And I have to tell you that it's hard for people to understand those possible uses of capital, but we have so many exciting businesses to invest in. And Pro is a great example. I'm not going to get into specifics, Adam, but I think people will understand over time how many exciting opportunities there are at Ford. And I'm not just referring to vehicles, I'm referring to non-vehicle activities. We have 27,000 service base. There's lots of opportunities.

And as I said, when you look at Ford Pro, I'll just deep dive on that one, and I'm so glad you asked this question, by the way. So Ford Pro is a very, very high percentage of our company's profit. Just look at the ratio between the overall company's EBIT in Pro and our guidance. And then think about our after-sales business, only 24% of our revenue comes from Pro-related vehicles.

And we could spend a lot of time talking about this opportunity, but that's why I highlighted in my comments, there are so many places for us to grow Pro on the physical services. They're like 35% margin, but it's hard stuff, right? It's like bays and technicians and a lot of work. We have very exciting electrical architecture investments that we have to make in our future that will really change our ICE vehicles and their revenue potential. Because that revenue won't be just when we sell the vehicle, it will be over time, which we're seeing with Pro already.

So I don't want to belabor the point. I'll just tell you there are plenty of opportunities.

Now, I'm not saying that Ford shouldn't be investing in the future. It's just hard to know if it will pay off, and it's hurting the stock's value in the short term.

Reverse DCF Valuation

Here's another way of looking at its valuation using a reverse DCF calculator that I've created. For this calculator, I've used the forward free cash flow per share based on the midpoint of management's guidance, but I removed $497 million of SBC expense (SBC in the TTM is $497 million, so I'm assuming it will stay at that level for simplicity's sake). I discounted the forward cash flows back 6 months, and I used the basic share count of 3.975 billion. The cost of equity was calculated using the CAPM model, with a beta of 1.63 and an equity risk premium of 5%. I set the terminal growth rate to 2%, in line with long-term inflation expectations.

At the current price, that means the market is pricing in that Ford's free cash flow per share will fall by 5.325% every year for the next 10 years and then grow at 2% in perpetuity. Yes, admittedly, this seems very pessimistic. However, it could be justified, given the high competition and the need for Ford to maintain high CapEx to stay competitive -- not to mention that analysts expect FCF to fall. If you think Ford will surpass this growth rate, then it's undervalued, but I'm not willing to take the risk, especially with the auto market only expected to grow by 1-2% in 2025 and 2026, according to S&P Global.

The Takeaway

While Ford has a low valuation, there are challenges. Ford faces tough competition from Tesla and Chinese automakers like BYD, which harms Ford’s profit margins and its ability to operate a profitable EV business. Plus, I'm not a fan of its declining (and low) gross profit margin as well as its low and unpredictable ROE.

With earnings coming up, one of the things investors will be watching for is signs of improvement in its warranty reserves, which have been a drag on recent earnings. However, the lack of consistent earnings beats and poor post-earnings stock reactions make it a risky bet going into the report, in my view.

Moreover, even though Ford's dividend yield may seem appealing, I believe it’s not high enough relative to its cost of equity and the fact that the dividend growth prospects are not strong. Additionally, Ford’s decision to prioritize investments in areas like Ford Pro over share buybacks may benefit the company in the long term, but in the short term, it hurts the valuation.

While Ford’s management sees exciting opportunities ahead, including its focus on software and services through Ford Pro, it's hard to know if these efforts will pay off. With the factors mentioned above, combined with the high level of competition in the EV market and the need for significant capital investments, F stock may not be the best buy at the moment.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Happy 60th Anniversary, CAPM! Why The Capital Asset Pricing Model Still Matters
data.description:
When someone hears I’m currently writing the authorized biography of William (Bill) Sharpe, the most frequent question I get is, “Is he still alive?” Sharpe is the 1990 recipient of the Sveriges Riksbank Prize in Economic Sciences in Memory of Alfred Nobel, commonly known as the Nobel Prize in Economics. And, yes, in September 2024, he is still alive and well. He lives in Carmel-by-the-Sea in California. Every Thursday morning, he meets with his coffee klatch. He can often be seen walking his bichon-poodle near Carmel Bay. In June 2024, he celebrated his 90th birthday.

And September 2024 was another Sharpe milestone: the 60th anniversary of his seminal capital asset pricing model (CAPM) paper in The Journal of Finance. It is extremely rare for research to remain relevant after a decade, let alone six. I’ll explain what the paper is about, how it impacted the investment industry, most likely including your own portfolio, and why it still matters.

The C-A-P-M

Let’s talk about the model’s name, common acronym, and what it’s really about. First, Sharpe never called it the “capital asset pricing model.” As the title of his seminal article indicates, it’s about “capital asset prices.” Later researchers referred to it as a model, adding the M. Second, once it became known as the capital asset pricing model, it was referred to by the acronym CAPM, pronounced “cap-em.”

Virtually every finance professor and student refer to it as “cap-em” - everyone except Sharpe himself. He always uses the initialism C-A-P-M. (So, if you want to honor the creator of the model, you can refer to it as the C-A-P-M!) Third, the focus isn’t really about prices of assets, but rather their expected returns. One of the key insights of the CAPM is that it answers an important investment question: “What is the expected return if I purchase security XYZ?”

Key Assumptions

Sharpe had written a paper published in 1963, “A Simplified Model for Portfolio Analysis,” that presented some of the same key concepts as in the seminal 1964 paper. There is an important difference between the two papers. As Sharpe later described it, in the 1963 paper, he carefully “put the rabbit in the hat” before pulling it out. The 1963 paper also answered that key question, “What is the expected return if I purchase security XYZ?”

But the rabbit he put in the hat was a preordained relationship between a security and the overall market - what I’ll describe later as beta. Andrew Lo and I interviewed Sharpe for our book, "In Pursuit of the Perfect Portfolio: The Stories, Voices, and Key Insights of the Pioneers Who Shaped the Way We Invest." “So, I spent several months trying to figure out how to do it without putting the rabbit in the hat,” he said. “Was there a way to pull the rabbit out of the hat without putting it in to begin with? I figured out yes, there was.” In the 1964 article, Sharpe didn’t put a rabbit in the hat, but rather, he derived a market equilibrium based on theory.

With any theory, you need to make assumptions, to simplify what happens in the real world, so that you can get traction with the theoretical model. That’s what Sharpe did. He assumed that all that investors care about are expected returns and risk. He assumed investors were rational and well-diversified. And he assumed investors could borrow and lend and the same rate.

When Sharpe initially submitted the paper for publication in The Journal of Finance, it was rejected, mainly because of Sharpe’s assumptions. The anonymous referee concluded that the assumptions Sharpe had made were so “preposterous” that all subsequent conclusions were “uninteresting.” Undeterred, two years later Sharpe made some paper tweaks, found a new editor, and the paper was published. The rest, as they say, is history.

The CAPM in Pictures

Much of Sharpe’s classic paper focuses on nine figures or graphs. The first seven are in two-dimensional space, with risk - as measured by the standard deviation of expected returns - on the vertical axis and expected return on the horizontal axis. (Any finance student will quickly note that the now-common practice is to flip axes, which is to represent risk on the horizontal axis and expected return on the vertical axis.)

On his horizontal axis, Sharpe began with the return on a special security that he called the “pure interest rate,” or P. Today, we would refer to that special rate as the Treasury Bill return, or the risk-free rate, commonly represented as Rf.

The curve igg’ is Harry Markowitz’s efficient frontier: the “optimal” combination of risky securities such that each portfolio on the curve has the highest expected return for a given level of risk, and also the lowest risk for a given level of expected return. Sharpe’s model essentially looked for combinations of the risk-free security, P, with each portfolio on the curve igg’ that would provide the optimal risk-expected return. It is clear from the graph that the optimal mix is formed by a line from P that is tangent to curve igg’ - in other words, the mix that combines the risk-free asset P and portfolio g.

In Sharpe’s world, we can think of the investor as essentially having three choices. She can invest all of her money in risky portfolio g. If that’s too much risk for her, she can divide her portfolio between combinations of risk-free P and risky g. Or, if she wants even more risk she can borrow at the risk-free rate and invest more than 100% of her wealth in risky g, essentially moving along the line toward Z. The line PgZ is Sharpe’s famous Capital Market Line, showing the optimal combination of risk-free and risky investments, including either lending (buying a Treasury Bill) or borrowing (at the Treasury Bill rate).

The Footnote that Won a Nobel Prize

After presenting a series of graphs, Sharpe showed how this could lead to “a relatively simple formula which relates the expected rate of return to various elements of risk for all assets which are included in combination g.” He then refers the reader to his footnote 22, an extensive 17 lines of equations and text that may be one of the most consequential footnotes in all of finance and economics literature.

That last line of the footnote may not look familiar, but with a bit of sleight of hand it will come into focus. Sharpe gave the left hand side a new name: Big, with “ig” as the subscript. In technical terms, Big is the covariance of the return on security i relative to security g, divided by the standard deviation of g. When creating the manuscript, Sharpe used a typewriter, with standard keys. What he really meant by B was the Greek letter b or beta. And as we’ll see, that has become one of the most used measures of risk today.

What Drives Expected Returns?

One of the key insights from Sharpe’s model is that when it comes to a security’s expected return, all that matters is Big, or beta.

In Sharpe’s final graph, expected return is still on the horizontal axis, but his new measure of risk, Big or beta, is on the vertical axis. Now the line PQ is actual the CAPM equation. What it powerfully shows is that, assuming an investor holds a well-diversified portfolio, the only measure of risk that matters is beta, or how risky the security is relative to the overall portfolio g. Since all investors want to hold g, then it must contain all assets. In other words, it must be the market portfolio. Today, we call that portfolio M.

We can now re-write Sharpe’s original derivation of the CAPM to the more familiar version: E(Ri) = Rf + b x [E(Rm) – Rf] or E(Ri) = Rf + bi x MRP, where i represents security i and MRP is the market risk premium. Here’s the intuition. Let’s suppose you’re considering investing in a stock for the next 10 years - or maybe not. Alternatively, you could invest in long-term Treasuries and secure a return of Rf. Or you could invest in the market as a whole and get an expected return of E(Rm). That works out to be the same as Rf + MRP. Or finally, you could invest in security i. Your expected return, E(Ri) would be driven by how much market risk you are exposed to, bi.

Beta has a simple interpretation: how risky a particular security is relative to the overall market. In terms of benchmarks, by definition “the market” has a beta of 1.0. For a particular security, beta suggests what the particular return change is for every 1.0% change in the market. For example, for a low-risk stock with a beta of 0.5, if the market (often proxied as the S&P 500 Index) goes up by 1.0 percent, we would expect stock i to go up by 0.5 percent; if the market is down by 1.0%, we expect stock i to go down by 0.5 percent. The same logic holds for a risky stock, say with a beta of 1.5. If the market goes up by 1.0%, we would expect stock i to go up by 1.5%. If the market is down by 1.0 percent, we expect stock i to go down by 1.5%.

Why the CAPM Still Matters

Sharpe’s seminal 1964 paper matters for three reasons:

  1. Beta is the appropriate measure of risk for a stock that is part of a diversified portfolio. It is also a widely available measure on sites such as Yahoo Finance. All that matters is risk relative to the market. If you have a diversified portfolio, it doesn’t matter how volatile a stock is on its own.
  2. Sharpe’s model, and in some sense Figure 7, shows us a way to measure performance across well-diversified portfolios such as mutual funds. We can measure a fund’s performance or return, say over the past five years, in excess of what a risk-free investment would have returned. That’s the return measure. If we compare that to the fund’s risk as measured by the standard deviation of the fund’s return over that period, we have a return-to-risk measure. That’s what Sharpe described in subsequent research papers and became known as the Sharpe ratio. It’s probably the most common measure of performance today.
  3. In Sharpe’s CAPM paper, he defined his special portfolio, g, the one that everyone would want to hold, as one that represented “all assets.” That’s why we call it the market portfolio. In a narrower interpretation, it should at least contain all stocks. Specific to the United States, that implies buying an index fund like one that replicates the S&P 500 Index. We have Sharpe’s model to thank for the multi-trillion dollar index fund that has emerged over the past 50 years. Chances are that you’re invested in an index fund, either directly or indirectly, say through a pension fund.

Of course, CAPM has its critics. There are some competing models of expected return that capture additional factors beyond the market. There are some questionable empirical test results. And yet, the model is still front and center in finance courses and still used by practitioners. And it’s a very intuitive model. It has stood the test of time.

So please join me in wishing the CAPM a happy birthday, with many more to come!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: MicroVision's Financing Strategy: Understanding The Risks Of The Convertible Note
data.description:
Background

MicroVision (NASDAQ:MVIS) released two updates last week, one describing preliminary revenue of $150 to $200K for Q3 and its cash condition with $43M on hand plus the $38M received from the planned financial transaction of the convertible note. The company's financial situation was given as follows:

After putting into effect to the net proceeds from the first $45.0 million tranche of the financing transaction, we expect to have approximately $81.0 million in cash and cash equivalents and access to $153 million of additional capital, including $123 million under our existing ATM, or at-the-market, facility and $30 million from the remaining commitment pursuant to the convertible note facility.

In addition, the company reaffirmed its revenue forecast of $8 to $10M, which would leave $5 to $7M to be delivered in Q4. Finally, the cash burn run rate was established at $55 to $60M, which gives the end of 2025 for the existing cash of $81M.

I focused on the second release and Key Terms of the Convertible Notes, which I am quoting below:

"The securities purchase agreement executed on October 14, 2024 provides for, upon closing, the sale from MicroVision to note holders of $45 million in senior secured convertible notes and provides for, at subsequent closings, the future sale, at the Company's option and subject to certain conditions, including that MicroVision has an effective resale registration statement on file for all shares underlying the Notes purchased at the initial closing and a requirement to seek stockholder approval, of up to an additional $30 million of senior secured convertible notes (collectively, the "Notes"). The Notes were issued with an original issue discount of 8%, generally convert to common stock at $1.596 per share, and will mature on October 1, 2026. Subject to certain conditions, the Company has the right to convert the Notes at any time if the closing sale price of the Company's common stock has been equal to at least 150% of the conversion price for the last 20 consecutive trading days. The Company has agreed to customary registration rights with respect to the resale of any shares of common stock issued upon conversion. If not converted, the Notes must be repaid at 110% of the face amount, with partial repayments beginning, at the option of the note holder, on January 1, 2025. The closing of the transaction remains subject to customary closing conditions."

To summarize, the note cost MicroVision an 8% issued discount of $3.6M plus fees of $2.95M; the company declared to receive $38M.

Using the SEC filings to obtain more information, I sought more details. Below is my analysis.

Convertible Note Analysis

The last reported sale price on the date of the Resale Registration Statement (yet to be filed in the coming weeks) is critical to determining the initial principal amounts considered for converting the notes. If the stock price is $1.33 or more, then $5.2M becomes convertible for payments from January to March 2025. If the price is below $1.33 on that date, the conversion amount becomes $12.5M and is pushed to July. The initial principal payments reduce the $45M, and the outstanding amount now converts at $1.5960 if that is the price or higher before a conversion date.

Suppose the price of shares is below the conversion price; in that case, notes are payable back in cash to return the principal amount and 10% interest. The CFO confirmed this in the statement given in the update to shareholders on October 18th.

The partial redemption payments fluctuate from up to $1.925M monthly from January to March 2025 to as much as $3.85M from April 2025 to October 2026.

I am quoting the entire paragraph from the SEC filing here to determine the conversion rate.

“ If the Holder elects to convert the Convertible Note with respect to the Initial Principal Amount underlying the Initial Partial Redemption Payments, the conversion price will be an amount equal to (i) one thousand dollars ($1,000) divided by (ii) the “first conversion rate,” which is an amount equal to one hundred ten percent (110%) of a fraction whose numerator is one thousand dollars ($1,000) and whose denominator is the lesser of (A) $1.5960, which is equal to one hundred twenty percent (120%) of the last reported sale price on October 14, 2024 and (B) ninety percent (90%) of the Nasdaq Minimum Price (as defined in Nasdaq Rule 5635(d)) as of the effective date of the Resale Registration Statement (as defined in the Securities Purchase Agreement) to be filed in connection with the Initial Purchased Notes subject to customary anti-dilution adjustments.”

To help understand this detail is the Nasdaq minimum price definition:

Listing Rule 5635(d) defines "Minimum Price" as the lower of (i) the closing price (as reflected on Nasdaq.com) or (ii) the average closing price of the common stock (as reflected on Nasdaq.com) for the five trading days immediately preceding the signing of the binding agreement.

For example, if the five-day average is $1.20 before that date, this becomes the minimum price because it is lower than $1.34.

Now, here is the formula for the conversion rate and price.

For the illustration, let's assume the minimum price is $1.20. Since $1.20 is lower than $1.5960, 90% of $1.20 would be used as $1.08 in the conversion rate calculation. The calculation is as follows: 1.10 x 1000/1.08 =1.10 x 925.2=1018.52

The conversion price would be $1000/1018.52 = $0.98

The maximum conversion price is $1.45 per share. The 90% of the minimum price of $1.78 is $1.60, and $1.5960 becomes the denominator, which has $1.45 in that outcome. So, the conversion is capped at the top but opened at the bottom.

The max conversion of $1.45 at a share price of $1.77 still results in a 22% gain. In that case, $5.2M would only be converted if the last record sale exceeds $1.33, and the rest would convert by $1.5960.

Worst Case Scenario

The situation gets muddier when prices are below $1.5690.

If $1.12 is the minimum price using the Nasdaq rule, the conversion price becomes $0.916, and the amount to be converted is now $12.5M. MicroVision sells 13.6M shares, resulting in a 22% profit for the convertible note holder.

The holder could have sold the same number of shares above $1.12 short, but assuming they all sold at the same price, this would have resulted in a $2.7M profit.

At that point, $32.5M of the convertible note requires over $1.5960 for conversion, or the holder can redeem the capital and 10% interest as a return in cash. 10% is $3.25M, which adds to a total of $35.75M.

Given the risk of price fluctuation, MicroVision must maintain this cash if the price is lower and there is an apparent set of covenants covering this requirement for minimum cash.

In the worst-case scenario, $35.75M would be returned, including the interest, a deduction from the initial $38M received. As a result, the company handed 13.6M shares for a balance of $2.2M.

The deal for the note holder is essentially risk-free. They make 10% on payment if the conversion price is unmet and 22% on the initial amount at a minimum of $5.2M to $12.5M value converted into shares. They have got an 8% discount on $45M.

Covenants protect the holder, as MicroVision needs to keep its cash account ready for cash payments when the price is below the conversion level. The $38M may appear as a quick boost to a balance sheet when seen in Q4, but it is so dependent on the share price, in my opinion, that the risk outweighs the effect it creates on it.

Optimists may argue that the company is confident in the share price's potential for growth due to future yet undefined deals. Still, I cannot think of a financial transaction built on a share price higher than the current one, resulting in dilution. The effect is the opposite; the share price loses value upon dilution. The conclusion is that while the first leg of the deal is very dilutive, confirming that this is a regular sale of the equity at a discount to the buyer, the second part of the deal requires stock to perform, or the money needs to be returned. And that part is entirely out of the company's control and, frankly, weird to be seen in the transaction.

MicroVision has a $123M ATM; selling the equity would have been much more effective and sustainable, which is what MicroVision has done for most of its history to keep its operation going. The note holder cannot lose; what does MicroVision win?

The noted update to shareholders has highlighted that the company's key focus is shifting toward entering the industrial segment. At the same time, consumer auto ADAS wins are anticipated for as late as 2028 or 2029. Over the next five years, this effort will likely require a $150M reserve already described in the news, funded by a combination of an ATM facility and a second $30M tranche of a convertible note.

What struck me as odd was the mention of revenue from these industrial wins being treated as cash, with no associated production costs, seemingly flowing straight to the bottom line. I consider this a result of casual discussion, considering MicroVision's gross margin for 2024 to date is just 1%, or $25K on $2.8M revenue—clearly, revenue is not cash. While the company's efforts to pitch MOVIA sensors to 15 customers seem promising, the revenue outcome or the cost of those sales remains undefined.

Conclusion

Management may have been caught off guard by the market's reaction. The stock has dropped 16% since the announcement, and as a result, I believe the update was put together to reassure investors. It did not have the desired effect. If deals are announced, the stock will go up, but the timeline of the deals has aged for over a year now, and so is the narrative.

I remain skeptical. In my last article, published in May, I maintained a 'sell' rating on MicroVision, citing my doubts about the product line and my concerns about the company's inability to secure deals. Five months later, nothing has changed, except that the expected deal timeline has now been pushed to 2029. While I highlighted the need for financing, I believe the convertible note in its current form is counterproductive. It introduces debt to the company's balance sheet and imposes covenants that limit financial flexibility. The conversion price poses a significant challenge, potentially costing the company cash to pay back the principal and 10% interest if unmet. If deals are indeed on the horizon, the ATM alone could sustain the company. My "sell" rating remains with the added financial risk from the convertible note.

data.title: 5 Stocks I'm Buying As I Reexamined My Sphere Of Competence
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of my weekly articles covering a wide array of topics relevant to and (hopefully) insightful for investors, especially dividend investors.

There's too much meat and potatoes to cover this week to waste time on appetizers, so let's tie the proverbial napkin round our necks and get ready to feast.

Here's what's on the menu:

  • An examination of investor spheres of competence, and how I am attempting to refocus on my own competency
  • Why I think the S&P 500 is a bad place to allocate capital right now, even though it could absolutely surge higher
  • Various topics that I believe are worth your time:
  • Why the "soft landing vs. hard landing" debate matters
  • Real-time inflation is below pre-COVID levels
  • How are consumers doing?
  • Market leadership has shifted from growth to dividend stocks
  • The BBC's straightforward, fact-based take on the economic effects of tariffs
  • My dividend stock buy list

Onward.

Investor Spheres of Competence

I've got a characteristically liberal arts brain. I am interested in and have a little bit of knowledge about a lot of various topics.

That little bit of knowledge is often enough to tempt me into thinking I'm more of an expert in a given field than I really am.

In truth, because of my work background, my core proficiency and competency is in the real estate industry. I've been a real estate agent in the past, I've worked at a commercial real estate investment company, and my longest running individual stock investments have been real estate investment trusts ("REITs").

I understand the real estate business -- its strengths and opportunities as well as its deficiencies and limitations.

Real estate instilled in me a love of cash flow. I bring that cash flow mindset with me into my stock investing.

The stock market isn't one game with the same rules that everyone plays against each other. It's a game that's broad and diverse enough to be winnable multiple ways.

Investors should play the game the way they are most likely to win it.

For me, that means focusing on my sphere of competency in my individual stock picks and gaining exposure to all other areas through well-constructed and diversified dividend ETFs.

When it comes to competency, I envision two concentric spheres:

  • Inner Ring: A deep understanding of the industry and company
  • Outer Ring: Thorough knowledge of publicly available information but no informational or intuitional edge

Here's how I view my own concentric spheres of competency:

CORE PROFICIENCY (INNER RING) Share of Portfolio
Commercial Real Estate & REITs 44%
WORKING COMPETENCY (OUTER RING)
Utilities & Independent Power Producers 15%
Business Development Companies ("BDCs") 11%

Midstream Energy

 8%

A Select Few Regional Banks

 3%

As you can see, about 81% of my portfolio is invested in individual stocks (or sector-specific ETFs like the Global X MLP & Energy Infrastructure ETF (MLPX)) that I would consider to be within my spheres of competency.

Another 9.5% of my portfolio is invested in two diversified dividend ETFs: the Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) and the iShares Core High Dividend ETF (HDV).

Over time, I'd like to grow my exposure to these ETFs (and a few others I do not yet own) so that they represent 30-50% of my portfolio instead of ~10%.

The remaining ~10% of my portfolio that falls outside of my spheres of competency are non-real estate stocks like Verizon (VZ), Comcast (CMCSA), and Brookfield Asset Management (BAM), which are special cases where I think I bought them at attractive enough cost bases to (hopefully) overcome my lack of informational edge in their respective industries.

However, if and when these names reach full value (or become overvalued, in my view), I would consider selling them to reinvest into either stocks in my sphere of competency or a diversified dividend ETF like SCHD.

Why The S&P 500 Is A Bad Investment Right Now

I of course have no idea what the stock market is going to do in the future. I don't have a crystal ball.

But for valuation reasons, now looks to me like a bad time to be allocating incremental capital to a broad-based index like the S&P 500 (SPY).

Before you scroll down to the comments and tell me not to time the market, let me show you some charts.

Here's one that shows now only the forward P/E ratio (at its third-highest level in the last three decades), but also a number of other valuation metrics like dividend yield, price-to-book, and price-to-cash flow -- all meaningfully above their 30-year average.

Using any valuation metric you prefer, the stock market looks richly valued right now.

Could it get even more richly valued? Sure.

But let's look at the historical record. Historically, when the SPY is valued like it is today, forward 1-year and 5-year returns are pretty poor.

The band of possible outcomes is pretty wide, but as you can see, the average outcome of future returns based on current valuations is basically 0% over the next year and the low single-digits annually over the next 5 years.

Yikes.

But wait, isn't the market's high valuation just attributable to the largest mega-cap names in the index?

Not really.

Here's the S&P 500's valuations broken out between the top 10 stocks in the index versus the bottom 490.

It is true that the top 10 stocks are valued much higher than their three-decade average, but the rest of the SPY is also above its long-term average -- 17% as of the end of Q3 2024.

Here's another clue to the SPY's generally unattractive valuation right now: Insiders aren't buying.

Corporate insider buying is around the lowest level that it generally gets, and this low level of insider buying is generally seen during periods right before a rollover in stock price performance.

This doesn't necessarily mean that history will repeat. But you have to admit, it's not exactly an encouraging sign for further appreciation in the SPY.

Worth Your Time

In what follows, I'll cover a variety of topics I believe are worth investors' time in as rapid-fire a manner as possible.

Why The "Soft Landing Vs. Hard Landing" Debate Matters

The debate about whether the US economy is headed toward a near-future recession or not matters both to the amount of Fed cuts likely to manifest over the next year as well as the performance of stocks going forward.

Historically, the forward performance of the S&P 500 (SPY) after the first Fed rate cut has been mixed, and it all depends on whether the economy is headed for a near-term recession or not.

While we did have a brief but deep recession during the initial phase of the COVID-19 pandemic, the sheer amount of fiscal and monetary stimulus that accompanied it basically created a soft landing for markets in the 2019 Fed rate cuts case.

But there was no recession in the 2 years following the 1984 and 1995 cutting cycles, and the 1990 recession following the 1989 Fed rate cuts was relatively shallow and brief. The S&P 500 dipped and completely rebounded to its previous level in about 6 months.

Of all of the above historical examples, I think the most likely analog to today's situation is probably 1989.

I don't think we will see an extended selloff in tech stocks like we did after the 2000 Dot Com bubble burst. And I don't think a 2008-style Great Financial Crisis recession is in the cards, short of some Black Swan event that I, by definition, can't foresee.

Then again, history doesn't repeat, and it doesn't always rhyme in the way we think it will.

In any case, as you can see above in the chart on the right, long-term bonds have historically performed positively in virtually every case of Fed rate cuts.

And you know what is a good proxy for long-term bonds? You guessed it: REITs.

Real-Time Inflation Is Below Pre-COVID Levels

I've written about this several times before, as recently as in last week's article: "8 Dividend Stocks On My Buy List As Inflation Keeps Dropping."

The CPI's shelter component uses severely lagging data that takes about 12-15 months to build in changes in residential rent rates. What about home prices or mortgage rates? The CPI's shelter metrics don't account for those at all.

If the CPI used real-time changes in residential rent rates instead of the lagged shelter metrics, it would already show year-over-year inflation well below 2%. In fact, real-time inflation has been below 2% for over a year now.

As you can see, this is both on a headline and core (excluding food & energy) basis.

Does the Fed's rate-cutting cycle risk inciting another round of inflation?

Almost certainly not. In every Fed rate-cutting cycle going back to the early 1970s, the CPI has been lower at the end of the Fed rate-cutting cycle than it was at the start of it.

Again, this doesn't mean history always repeats or that something unique can't happen every now and then. But, historically speaking, inflation is likely to be lower at the end of the Fed rate cuts than it is now.

Consumers can apparently sense this, according to the University of Michigan's consumer sentiment survey. Expectations for year-ahead inflation have come down considerably, even though consumers are still struggling to handle the cumulative inflation that has occurred over the last several years.

My contention remains that the sources of high inflation from 2021 to 2023 were totally unique to COVID-19 and (especially) the government's fiscal response to it.

We have not entered some new era of secularly high inflation (at least not at this time). Eventually, the markets will realize this, and interest rates will come down further, although perhaps not to their previous lows due to the current level of government debt issuance.

Confusing Signals About Consumer Strength

Are consumers strong and likely to spend liberally this holiday season, or are they weakening and being forced to cut back on spending?

There are some confusing signals being sent.

For example, the CNBC/NRF's retail sales metric shows that retail sales dropped month-over-month in September.

But then a week later, the NRF released their own standalone data on retail sales for September, showing a seasonally adjusted month-over-month increase of 0.4% and 1.4% unadjusted YoY growth.

Huh?

Granted, they calculate retail sales a little differently, but it's tough to judge which one is more trustworthy.

Here are some more confusing signals.

Household debt service makes up about the same as it did immediately preceding COVID:

And yet, as you can see in the second chart above, credit card and auto loan delinquencies are rising and at their highest level since the GFC in 2008-2009.

If debt service is a reasonable level of disposable income, why are delinquencies on the rise?

I think the tyranny of averages is coming into play here. I suspect that the top 30-40% of households by income level are doing very well financially, while the bottom 60-70% of households are increasingly struggling. And the rate at which the top households are doing better and better is masking the rate at which the bottom households are doing worse and worse.

Here's another data point showing increasing stress under the surface: A rising share of consumer debt payments are likely to be missed in the next three months.

While some folks are clearly doing very well in this economy, others are clearly doing quite poorly.

Market Leadership Is Shifting Back To Dividend Growers

I've documented the shift in market leadership since the beginning of July this year from techy growth stocks to undervalued dividend-paying stocks.

Here's a chart illustrating this:

That chart still shows that dividend growers have underperformed the SPY over the last 12 months as of the end of Q3 2024, but zooming in to the period from early July to today, we find that all types of dividend stocks have been outperforming.

SCHD and the Vanguard High Dividend Yield ETF (VYM) have modestly outperformed, but the real winners have been the bond-proxy sectors like the Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ) and the SPDR Utilities ETF (XLU):

Real estate and especially utilities have been on fire since the beginning of July, and I think that outperformance should continue for the foreseeable future.

The BBC On The Impact of Tariffs

The BBC recently put out an article that I found to be arguably the best summary on the subject matter of US tariffs and their economic impact.

It's a straightforward and fact-based examination of the effects of the 2018-2019 tariffs as well as an analysis of the likely impacts of the further tariffs that have been proposed.

For example, the 2018-2019 tariffs (still in place today) did not successfully increase the number of US steel manufacturing jobs:

Nor did they discernibly affect the trade deficit:

And many analyses from various groups indicate the likelihood of further tariffs triggering some degree of inflation, which would disproportionately burden lower income households:

However, the market seems to have broadly come to peace with the idea of higher tariffs if for no other reason than that the potential future administration promising them is also promising deregulation, business-friendly leadership at the Securities and Exchange Commission ("SEC") and Federal Trade Commission ("FTC"), and no tax increases.

The Buy List

Here's where my buy list stands today.

Dividend Yield Projected Dividend Growth Rate (Guesstimate)
American Homes 4 Rent (AMH) 2.7% High-Single-Digit to Low-Double-Digit
Artesian Resources (ARTNA) 3.3% Mid-Single-Digit
Permian Resources (PR) 4.2% Mid-Single-Digit
Rexford Industrial (REXR) 3.7% Mid- to High-Single-Digit
Trinity Capital (TRIN) 14.8% Flat to Low-Single-Digit

I've written about each of these in the past, but I'd like to add some thoughts on two of them in particular.

First on AMH.

AMH isn't dirt cheap, but it's reasonably valued, in my view. And it's a wonderful business with a strong balance sheet, strong demographic tailwinds, and an underappreciated competitive advantage in its internal build-to-rent development arm.

Looking at price to cash from operations (very close to "funds from operations" for REITs), we find that AMH is trading below its 5-year average valuation.

Although the yield is low, long-term dividend growth investors could allocate their capital in much worse ways in today's market, in my opinion.

I think the combination of AMH's organic growth and external growth (from developments and acquisitions) will be a powerful one for the foreseeable future.

Second, REXR just reported its Q3 2024 earnings, and the market really didn't like what it saw. REXR's stock price dropped 9% the day after the earnings report dropped.

Actual occupancy (including redevelopment/repositioning assets) was down QoQ, rent growth is decelerating rapidly, and worst of all, market rent rates continue to decline QoQ.

As you can see above, market rent rates for SoCal infill industrial real estate fell another 2.5% QoQ and 7.5% YoY. That has caused REXR's mark-to-market (upside from in-place rents to the market rent rate) to drop from 38% in Q2 to 31% in Q3.

Not good.

Especially considering many analysts thought the decline in market rent rates in SoCal would halt and level off by now.

In the Q3 2024 earnings conference call, management partially blamed this leasing softness on economic and political uncertainty by saying that "current leasing activity reflects some tenants taking longer to make decisions."

On the plus side, management also stated that "assuming zero market rent growth, we currently project about 34% cash NOI growth embedded within our portfolio, realizable over the next three years."

If market rent rates hold steady from here and about 80% of that NOI growth drops down to AFFO, I would think REXR could still generate AFFO per share growth in the mid- to high-single-digits over the next three years.

In fact, Q3 still showed pretty nice AFFO per share growth on a YoY basis.

While REXR's core FFO per share increased 5.4% YoY, the REIT's AFFO per share of $0.50 in Q3 2024 was up 21% from $0.41 in Q3 2023, mostly due to a drop in capex costs (both redevelopment and re-tenanting expenses). But AFFO per share was down by a fraction of a point QoQ due to slightly higher capex costs from Q2 to Q3.

More good news is that REXR's debt maturity schedule looks pretty favorable over the next year or so.

Only a small fraction of debt matures through the end of 2025.

Even more good news is that supply headwinds have cooled rapidly since mid-2023. The construction pipeline of infill industrial projects in SoCal is lower today than it was in the years preceding COVID-19.

Given the fact that market rent rates have shown such weakness lately, I would surmise that the supply pipeline should continue to shrink.

In theory, the more pain REXR feels today from declining market rents, the less future supply competition it should face, as developers hold off on starting any new projects.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Weekly Indicators: More Hurricane Havoc
data.description:
Purpose

I look at the high frequency weekly indicators because while they can be very noisy, they provide a good nowcast of the economy, and will telegraph the maintenance or change in the economy well before monthly or quarterly data is available. They are also an excellent way to “mark your beliefs to market.” In general, I go in order of long leading indicators, then short leading indicators, then coincident indicators.

A Note on Methodology

Data is presented in a “just the facts, ma'am” format with a minimum of commentary so that bias is minimized.

Where relevant, I include 12-month highs and lows in the data in parentheses to the right. All data taken from St. Louis FRED unless otherwise linked.

A few items (e.g., Financial Conditions indexes, regional Fed indexes, stock prices, the yield curve) have their metrics based on long-term studies of their behavior.

Where data is seasonally adjusted, generally it is scored positively if it is within the top 1/3 of that range, negative in the bottom 1/3, and neutral in between. Where it is not seasonally adjusted, and there are seasonal issues, waiting for the YoY change to change sign will lag the turning point. Thus, I make use of a convention: data is scored neutral if it is less than 1/2 as positive/negative as at its 12-month extreme.

With long leading indicators, which by definition turn at least 12 months before a turning point in the economy as a whole, there is an additional rule: data is automatically negative if, during an expansion, it has not made a new peak in the past year, with the sole exception that it is scored neutral if it is moving in the right direction and is close to making a new high.

For all series where a graph is available, I have provided a link to where the relevant graph can be found.

Recap of monthly reports

September data included an increase in both nominal and real retail sales, but a decline in industrial production, housing starts and permits, although single family permits did increase slightly. All of these data points may have been affected by Hurricane Helene.

.

Long leading indicators

Interest rates and credit spreads

Rates

  • BAA corporate bond index 5.62%, down -0.01% w/w (1-yr range: 5.33-6.80)
  • 10-year Treasury bonds 4.08%, down -0.01% w/w (3.66-4.98)
  • Credit spread 1.54%, unchanged w/w (1.36-2.42).

(Graph at Moody's Seasoned Baa Corporate Bond Yield | FRED | St. Louis Fed.)

Yield curve

  • 10 year minus 2 year: +0.13%, down -0.01% w/w (-1.07 - +0.15)
  • 10 year minus 3 month: -0.56%, down -0.11 w/w (-1.65 - 0.62)
  • 2 year minus Fed funds: -0.88%, unchanged w/w.

(Graph at 10-Year Treasury Constant Maturity Minus 2-Year Treasury Constant Maturity | FRED | St. Louis Fed.)

30-Year conventional mortgage rate (from Mortgage News Daily) (graph at link)

  • 6.68%, up +0.04% w/w (6.09-8.03).

Long-term bonds’ have been neutral since the end of February. Both corporate and Treasury’s turned positive over one month ago, making new 1 year lows. But with their increases this week, both reverted to the neutral portion of their range. Additionally, the 3 month and the 2 year vs. 10 year Treasury spreads are still both close to or at their respective 12-month lows. The 10 vs. 2 year spread remains normalized, but not enough to warrant a change from neutral to positive.

Housing

Mortgage applications (from the Mortgage Bankers Association)

  • Purchase apps down -7% to 139 (125-162) [SA]
  • Purchase apps 4 wk avg. down -2 to 146 [SA]
  • Purchase apps YoY +7% [NSA]
  • Purchase apps YoY 4 wk avg. +6.5% [NSA] (12 month best)
  • Refi apps down -26% w/w [SA]
  • Refi apps YoY up +111% [SA].

*[SA] = seasonally adjusted, [NSA] = not seasonally adjusted.

(Graph at Our Charts.)

Real Estate Loans (from the FRB)

  • Up +0.1% w/w
  • Up +2.0% YoY (1.9% — 11.9%).

(Graph at Real Estate Loans, All Commercial Banks | FRED | St. Louis Fed.)

Mortgage rates, like bond yields, made multi-decade new highs late last year. Three weeks ago, they made a new 12-month low, turning positive. Their rates spiked high enough one week ago to change their rating back to neutral.

Purchase mortgage applications last made a new long term last year at this time. They have meandered since, warranting a neutral rating. Three weeks they finally turned higher YoY, and so are positive. Refinancing has traced a similar trajectory, recently turning significantly higher YoY by enough to warrant a positive rating.

Late last year, real estate loans sank below 1/2 of their 12-month-high, the last housing indicator to turn negative, and have generally continued to worsen. They paused for several months, but in the past two months made several YoY lows, and another one last week.

Money supply

The Federal Reserve has discontinued this weekly series. Data is now only released monthly. August data was released three weeks ago:

  • M1 m/m up +0.4%, YoY Real M1 down -3.3% (worst: -14.4% 8/23)(24 month YoY high)
  • M2 m/m up +0.6%, YoY Real M2 down -0.6% (worst -8.6% 9/23)(2 year+ YoY high).

No recession has happened without a YoY real M1 negative, or YoY real M2 below +2.5%. Real M2 fell below that threshold in March 2022. Real M1 also turned negative as of May 2022.

Although both of these are still negative, they are both again the “least bad” they have been since early 2022. On an absolute level, the post-pandemic bottom for real M1 was in February; for real M2 it was last October). In the past, when either has turned positive YoY, that was near either the middle or end of recessions, which would warrant a change to positive.

Corporate profits (Q2 actual + estimated) from I/B/E/S via FactSet at p. 29)

  • Q2 2024 actual unchanged at 60.55, up +7.3% q/q
  • Q3 2024 estimated down -1.26 to 60.07, down -0.8% q/q.

FactSet estimates earnings, which are replaced by actual earnings as they are reported, and are updated weekly. The “neutral” band is +/-3%. I also average the previous two quarters together, until at least 100 companies have actually reported. This metric was negative in Q4 2023, turned neutral in Q1, and very positive in Q2. Averaging in Q3, profits remain higher by 3.3%, so the rating remains positive. Next week Q2 will probably go out of the average, and if earnings remain negative q/q, that will cause this rating to change to neutral or negative.

Credit conditions (from the Chicago Fed) (graph at link)

  • Financial Conditions Index down -0.01 (looser) to -0.57 (-0.32 - -0.58)
  • Adjusted Index (removing background economic conditions) down -0.02 (looser) to -0.57 (-0.26 - -0.57) (new 12 month low)
  • Leverage subindex unchanged (loose) at -0.13 (+0.05 - -0.65).

In these indexes, lower = better for the economy. The Chicago Fed's Adjusted Index's real break-even point is roughly -0.25. In the leverage index, a negative number is good, a positive poor.
